user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. Plaintiff alleges total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and claims a refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of money with interest and costs

missing_fields: Specific details of the business transaction
assumptions: Jurisdiction is proper based on Defendant's residence or cause of action location
Suit is within the limitation period

must be read subject to its provisions. Under s 8 of Suits Valuation Act, 1887 the valuation for court fees and 
the valuation for jurisdiction in certain suits is the same. Under s 7 cl (iv)(f) of the Court Fees Act, 1870, the 
valuation for purposes of court fees in a suit for accounts lies in the discretion of the plaintiff. Therefore, in a suit 
for accounts, the plaintiffs valuation in the plaint fixes the jurisdiction of the court. 2 In a suit for unsettled 
accounts, fixed court fee is payable and for jurisdictional purposes, such a suit can be valued in terms of 
valuation as fixed by the plaintiff. 3 In a suit for partition, the value for the purpose of jurisdiction is the value of 
the share claimed by the plaintiff and not the value of the entire estate in which the partition is sought. 4 In a suit 
for recovery of property from the tenant whose tenancy has been terminated, the valuation of suit for 
jurisdictional purposes should be on the basis of annual rent or profit. Suit so valued along with the claim of 
damage exceeding pecuniary jurisdiction of the court is liable to be returned for prosecution before the proper 
court.5

rejected under this rule, even after it has been numbered and registered as a suit. The reason is that the power to 
reject a plaint under this rule is not exhausted when the plaint has been admitted and registered.8

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is the defendant herein and is sui juris and competent to be sued.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text: The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} is invoked in this money-recovery suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. The advance of Rs 20,00,000 was paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} from the Plaintiff's account with {{BANK_NAME}} {{BANK_BRANCH}} and credited to the Defendant in respect of the contract evidenced by Annexure A. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_OF_EXECUTION}}. The cause of action arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay the amount on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and thereby caused substantial loss to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff seeks return of Rs 20,00,000 with interest and costs; the amount is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court, whose monetary limit for such claims is Rs {{PECUNIARY_LIMIT}}. Annexures as referenced above are relied upon to establish the contractual relationship and payment. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant came together for a proposed business undertaking, and on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} they entered into a written contract for the execution of the project, the terms of which are set out in Annexure A. By the contract, the Plaintiff agreed to advance Rs 2,000,000 to the Defendant to facilitate the project, and the Defendant undertook to apply the funds to the project and to perform the agreed obligations by the timeline specified in the agreement.

2. The Agreement provided that the Plaintiff would make an advance of Rs 2,000,000 to the Defendant, to be paid on the date specified in the contract and evidenced by Annexure B. It also recorded the Defendant’s obligation to commence and complete the agreed work by the date identified as {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and to refund the advance if the performance failed or could not be completed as agreed.

3. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 2,000,000 to the Defendant’s designated account, by bank transfer, as evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B. The funds were received by the Defendant and acknowledged in the payment documentation.

4. Based on the express terms of Annexure A, the Plaintiff relied on the Defendant’s promise to perform the project within the specified timeframe and to utilize the funds for the project. The Plaintiff understood that the advancement would be applied toward the project’s execution and that the Defendant would deliver the agreed goods/services or other performance by the date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the obligations by the agreed date, and there was no refund of the Rs 2,000,000 advanced to the Defendant. The Defendant did not undertake or complete the project as contemplated, nor did the Defendant return the advance amount despite the Plaintiff’s expectations and reliance on the expressed terms of the agreement.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant on {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs 2,000,000, along with any applicable costs and interest, as set out in Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the contents of which are reflected in Annexure D. Thereafter, on {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff filed this suit seeking recovery of the advance amount with costs and such interest as may be lawfully due, together with any other relief deemed appropriate by this Honourable Court. The cause of action arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and the Plaintiff reserves the right to rely on further communications and documents as may be necessary, including those listed in Annexure E and Annexure F.

Annexure references: Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice), Annexure D (Reply to legal notice), Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission document, if any), Annexure F (Additional supporting documents).

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text: Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a restitutionary obligation whereby if money or property has been paid or delivered under an agreement or contract that is found void or becomes void, the person who has received such advantage is bound to restore it to the person from whom it was received. In the present case, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} paid Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} as an advance under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{LOCATION}}, as evidenced by Annexure B (Payment proof) and Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document); the business transaction contemplated by the Agreement did not materialize because the Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to perform or repay the advance, despite a demand communicated by the Plaintiff in the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} (Annexure C) and the Defendant’s reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D). This sequence amounts to total failure of consideration since the Plaintiff did not receive the contracted benefit, and the Defendant has retained the Rs. 2,000,000 without lawful justification; the Plaintiff is thus entitled to a refund of the principal amount with interest and costs, consistent with the legal consequence of restoration under Section 65. The action is within the three-year period under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, accrues on the date of failure {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, and the suit was timely filed on {{FILING_DATE}}; the Plaintiff addresses possible defenses by noting that there was no part performance by the Defendant, no forfeiture clause defeats the remedy, no adjustment has been made, and there is no counter-claim or set-off that would diminish the Plaintiff’s right to restitution; in the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff contends that, if Section 65 is not applicable for any reason, the Plaintiff remains entitled to restitution and damages consistent with the breach of the underlying contract, with Annexure A, Annexure B, Annexure C, and Annexure D cited in support.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text:
The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the amount of Rs. 2,000,000 advanced by the Plaintiff under the contract evidenced by Annexure A, resulting in total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, and thereby giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to seek a refund of the advance amount with interest and costs. The Plaintiff had, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, performed his obligation by placing the said sum in the contract, as evidenced by Annexure B. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served the legal notice demanding refund, and the Defendant, having received such notice, failed, neglected, and refused to refund the advance, as evidenced by Annexure C. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 2,000,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the Plaintiff's claim remains unsettled as of the date of filing {{FILING_DATE}} and shall continue until actual realization, with interest and costs as may be awarded.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: The present suit is within time. The Limitation Act, 1963 applies, and Article 47 of the Schedule thereto prescribes a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails; accrual occurs on the date of the failure, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. Accordingly, the limitation clock began on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, which is within three years from accrual, and is therefore within the period of limitation.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff’s suit is valued at Rs. 2,000,000, equal to the claim amount sought in the plaint and as stated in the filing on {{FILING_DATE}} before the competent court having jurisdiction. The court fee payable is calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act, and the amount due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} on a simple basis, arising out of the Defendant's failure to repay the amount due under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) and evidenced by the Plaintiff's payment records in Annexure B. The Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by not discharging the obligation in accordance with the contractual arrangement, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of its funds. The commercial nature of the transaction and the contractual understanding between the parties justify a mercantile rate of interest at 12% per annum, reflecting the Defendant's wrongful retention of money and the Plaintiff's consequent loss of use of the funds. This claim is supported by Annexure A and Annexure B and is consistent with customary mercantile practice for such commercial transactions.

The pendente lite interest claimed under Section 34 CPC is on the same principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 and is sought at the rate of 12% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{PENDENTE_LITE_END_DATE}} on a simple basis. Section 34 CPC authorizes the Court to award interest on money during the pendency of the suit; the Plaintiff asserts that the Defendant had the benefit of the funds and the Plaintiff was deprived of use of the money during this period, justifying this relief in a commercial dispute. The claim is supported by Annexure C (Legal notice) and Annexure A (Agreement) along with Annexure B (Payment proof), and it aligns with the prevailing mercantile standards for such claims.

The Plaintiff also seeks future interest on the decretal amount post-decree on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at 12% per annum from {{DECREED_DATE}} to {{REALIZATION_DATE}} until realization, on a simple basis. The legal basis is the Court's power under Section 34 CPC to award interest on money until realization, reinforced by mercantile practice and any applicable contractual understanding. The rate reflects the commercial nature of the underlying transaction and the Defendant's ongoing delay in settlement; if the Court deems a different rate more appropriate, the Plaintiff requests that the Court determine the rate accordingly in line with the facts and law detailed herein. Annexure A and Annexure C substantiate the decretal basis and the ongoing obligation, and Annexure B provides supporting payment context where relevant.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:
(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and received by the Defendant. 
(b) By decree, the Defendant shall pay to the Plaintiff pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) The Court be pleased to award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} until the date of decree. 
(d) The Court be pleased to award future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) The Plaintiff prays for costs of the suit including the pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges of Rs. {{POSTAL_CHARGES}}/-, such costs amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-. 
(f) The Plaintiff prays for such other relief as the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

draft_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

BETWEEN Plaintiff and Defendant. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is the plaintiff herein and is sui juris and competent to sue. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is the defendant herein and is sui juris and competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} is invoked in this money-recovery suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. The advance of Rs 20,00,000 was paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} from the Plaintiff's account with {{BANK_NAME}} {{BANK_BRANCH}} and credited to the Defendant in respect of the contract evidenced by Annexure A. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_OF_EXECUTION}}. The cause of action arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay the amount on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and thereby caused substantial loss to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff seeks return of Rs 20,00,000 with interest and costs; the amount is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court, whose monetary limit for such claims is Rs {{PECUNIARY_LIMIT}}. Annexures as referenced above are relied upon to establish the contractual relationship and payment. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant came together for a proposed business undertaking, and on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} they entered into a written contract for the execution of the project, the terms of which are set out in Annexure A. By the contract, the Plaintiff agreed to advance Rs 2,000,000 to the Defendant to facilitate the project, and the Defendant undertook to apply the funds to the project and to perform the agreed obligations by the timeline specified in the agreement.

2. The Agreement provided that the Plaintiff would make an advance of Rs 2,000,000 to the Defendant, to be paid on the date specified in the contract and evidenced by Annexure B. It also recorded the Defendant’s obligation to commence and complete the agreed work by the date identified as {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and to refund the advance if the performance failed or could not be completed as agreed.

3. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 2,000,000 to the Defendant’s designated account, by bank transfer, as evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B. The funds were received by the Defendant and acknowledged in the payment documentation.

4. Based on the express terms of Annexure A, the Plaintiff relied on the Defendant’s promise to perform the project within the specified timeframe and to utilize the funds for the project. The Plaintiff understood that the advancement would be applied toward the project’s execution and that the Defendant would deliver the agreed goods/services or other performance by the date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the obligations by the agreed date, and there was no refund of the Rs 2,000,000 advanced to the Defendant. The Defendant did not undertake or complete the project as contemplated, nor did the Defendant return the advance amount despite the Plaintiff’s expectations and reliance on the expressed terms of the agreement.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant on {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of Rs 2,000,000, along with any applicable costs and interest, as set out in Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the contents of which are reflected in Annexure D. Thereafter, on {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff filed this suit seeking recovery of the advance amount with costs and such interest as may be lawfully due, together with any other relief deemed appropriate by this Honourable Court. The cause of action arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and the Plaintiff reserves the right to rely on further communications and documents as may be necessary, including those listed in Annexure E and Annexure F.

Annexure references: Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice), Annexure D (Reply to legal notice), Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission document, if any), Annexure F (Additional supporting documents).


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a restitutionary obligation whereby if money or property has been paid or delivered under an agreement or contract that is found void or becomes void, the person who has received such advantage is bound to restore it to the person from whom it was received. In the present case, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} paid Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} as an advance under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{LOCATION}}, as evidenced by Annexure B (Payment proof) and Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document); the business transaction contemplated by the Agreement did not materialize because the Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to perform or repay the advance, despite a demand communicated by the Plaintiff in the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} (Annexure C) and the Defendant’s reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D). This sequence amounts to total failure of consideration since the Plaintiff did not receive the contracted benefit, and the Defendant has retained the Rs. 2,000,000 without lawful justification; the Plaintiff is thus entitled to a refund of the principal amount with interest and costs, consistent with the legal consequence of restoration under Section 65. The action is within the three-year period under Article 47 of the Limitation Act, accrues on the date of failure {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, and the suit was timely filed on {{FILING_DATE}}; the Plaintiff addresses possible defenses by noting that there was no part performance by the Defendant, no forfeiture clause defeats the remedy, no adjustment has been made, and there is no counter-claim or set-off that would diminish the Plaintiff’s right to restitution; in the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff contends that, if Section 65 is not applicable for any reason, the Plaintiff remains entitled to restitution and damages consistent with the breach of the underlying contract, with Annexure A, Annexure B, Annexure C, and Annexure D cited in support.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to repay the amount of Rs. 2,000,000 advanced by the Plaintiff under the contract evidenced by Annexure A, resulting in total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, and thereby giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to seek a refund of the advance amount with interest and costs. The Plaintiff had, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, performed his obligation by placing the said sum in the contract, as evidenced by Annexure B. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served the legal notice demanding refund, and the Defendant, having received such notice, failed, neglected, and refused to refund the advance, as evidenced by Annexure C. The cause of action is a continuing one because the Defendant continues to retain the Plaintiff's sum of Rs. 2,000,000 without any lawful authority or justification, and the Plaintiff's claim remains unsettled as of the date of filing {{FILING_DATE}} and shall continue until actual realization, with interest and costs as may be awarded.

---SECTION_TEXT---


LIMITATION

The present suit is within time. The Limitation Act, 1963 applies, and Article 47 of the Schedule thereto prescribes a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails; accrual occurs on the date of the failure, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. Accordingly, the limitation clock began on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, which is within three years from accrual, and is therefore within the period of limitation.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The Plaintiff’s suit is valued at Rs. 2,000,000, equal to the claim amount sought in the plaint and as stated in the filing on {{FILING_DATE}} before the competent court having jurisdiction. The court fee payable is calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act, and the amount due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} on a simple basis, arising out of the Defendant's failure to repay the amount due under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A) and evidenced by the Plaintiff's payment records in Annexure B. The Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by not discharging the obligation in accordance with the contractual arrangement, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the use of its funds. The commercial nature of the transaction and the contractual understanding between the parties justify a mercantile rate of interest at 12% per annum, reflecting the Defendant's wrongful retention of money and the Plaintiff's consequent loss of use of the funds. This claim is supported by Annexure A and Annexure B and is consistent with customary mercantile practice for such commercial transactions.

The pendente lite interest claimed under Section 34 CPC is on the same principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 and is sought at the rate of 12% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} to {{PENDENTE_LITE_END_DATE}} on a simple basis. Section 34 CPC authorizes the Court to award interest on money during the pendency of the suit; the Plaintiff asserts that the Defendant had the benefit of the funds and the Plaintiff was deprived of use of the money during this period, justifying this relief in a commercial dispute. The claim is supported by Annexure C (Legal notice) and Annexure A (Agreement) along with Annexure B (Payment proof), and it aligns with the prevailing mercantile standards for such claims.

The Plaintiff also seeks future interest on the decretal amount post-decree on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at 12% per annum from {{DECREED_DATE}} to {{REALIZATION_DATE}} until realization, on a simple basis. The legal basis is the Court's power under Section 34 CPC to award interest on money until realization, reinforced by mercantile practice and any applicable contractual understanding. The rate reflects the commercial nature of the underlying transaction and the Defendant's ongoing delay in settlement; if the Court deems a different rate more appropriate, the Plaintiff requests that the Court determine the rate accordingly in line with the facts and law detailed herein. Annexure A and Annexure C substantiate the decretal basis and the ongoing obligation, and Annexure B provides supporting payment context where relevant.

---SECTION_TEXT---


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- (Rupees Two Million Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and received by the Defendant. 
(b) By decree, the Defendant shall pay to the Plaintiff pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. 
(c) The Court be pleased to award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} until the date of decree. 
(d) The Court be pleased to award future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree until realization. 
(e) The Plaintiff prays for costs of the suit including the pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges of Rs. {{POSTAL_CHARGES}}/-, such costs amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-. 
(f) The Plaintiff prays for such other relief as the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

BETWEEN Plaintiff and Defendant. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is the plaintiff herein and is sui juris and competent to sue. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is the defendant herein and is sui juris and competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} is invoked in this money-recovery suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. The advance of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- was paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} from the Plaintiff's account with {{BANK_NAME}} {{BANK_BRANCH}} and credited to the Defendant in respect of the business transaction/contract evidenced by Annexure A. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_OF_EXECUTION}}. The cause of action first arose when the transaction failed and the refund became due on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and continues due to the Defendant’s ongoing retention of the Plaintiff’s money. The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- with interest and costs; the suit valuation is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into discussions during {{NEGOTIATION_PERIOD}} for a proposed business transaction, namely {{TRANSACTION_DESCRIPTION}} (hereinafter “the Transaction”). Pursuant to such discussions, on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a written agreement/understanding ("Agreement"), the terms of which are evidenced by Annexure A.

2. Under the Agreement, the Plaintiff agreed to pay an advance of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Defendant towards/for the purpose of {{PURPOSE_OF_ADVANCE}}. The Defendant, in turn, undertook to perform/complete {{DEFENDANT_KEY_OBLIGATIONS}} within the agreed timelines, including by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, and to either (a) complete the Transaction and provide the agreed consideration/benefit to the Plaintiff, or (b) refund the advance if the Transaction did not materialize/failed in terms of the Agreement.

3. In performance of the Plaintiff’s obligation, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Defendant’s designated account by {{PAYMENT_MODE}} (bank transfer/NEFT/RTGS), as evidenced by Annexure B. The Defendant received the said amount.

4. After receipt of the advance, the Defendant was required to take steps including {{STEPS_REQUIRED_AFTER_ADVANCE}} and to complete performance by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff, acting on the Defendant’s assurances and the Agreement, awaited performance and was ready and willing to complete the Transaction as per the agreed terms.

5. However, on and from {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, the Defendant committed default by {{DEFAULT_PARTICULARS}}. As a result, the Transaction did not materialize and there was a total failure of the consideration/benefit which the Plaintiff was to receive under the Agreement, despite the Plaintiff having already paid the advance.

6. Upon the Transaction failing, the Plaintiff demanded refund of the advance from the Defendant on {{DEMAND_DATE}} by {{DEMAND_MODE}}. Thereafter, the Plaintiff caused a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to be issued to the Defendant demanding refund of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- along with interest and costs (Annexure C). The Defendant has failed and neglected to refund the said amount. Any reply sent by the Defendant, if applicable, is dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D).

Annexure references: Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice), Annexure D (Reply, if any), Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission document, if any), Annexure F (Additional supporting documents).


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

A. Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 (as specifically relied upon by the Plaintiff) provides, in substance, that when an agreement is discovered to be void or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement/contract is bound to restore it, or to make compensation for it, to the person from whom he received it.

B. In the present case, the Plaintiff paid Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Defendant as an advance under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A), as evidenced by Annexure B. The Transaction contemplated under the Agreement failed and the Plaintiff did not receive the agreed benefit/consideration due to the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} as pleaded above.

C. The Plaintiff pleads that, upon the Transaction failing and upon the Plaintiff’s termination/rescission/withdrawal from the Transaction on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} on account of the Defendant’s default, the Agreement/Transaction stood incapable of being performed/treated as having become void in the circumstances, for reasons including {{VOIDNESS_REASON}} / {{TERMINATION_RESCISSION_BASIS}}. Consequently, the Defendant, having received an advantage (Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-), is bound to restore the same to the Plaintiff under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

D. Without prejudice to the above Section 65 plea, and in the alternative, the Plaintiff is entitled to recovery of the said sum on principles of restitution/unjust enrichment and/or refund consequent to the Defendant’s breach of the Agreement, since the Defendant has retained the Plaintiff’s money despite total failure of the Transaction and despite demand.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform {{DEFENDANT_KEY_OBLIGATIONS}} and, consequently, the Transaction failed and the refund of the advance of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- became due and payable to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff had already paid the said sum on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as evidenced by Annexure B.

The cause of action further accrued on {{DEMAND_DATE}} and on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff demanded refund and caused issuance of legal notice (Annexure C), and the Defendant failed/neglected/refused to refund the amount.

The cause of action is continuing in nature because the Defendant continues to retain Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- belonging to the Plaintiff without lawful justification, and the Plaintiff’s claim remains unpaid as of the date of filing {{FILING_DATE}} and shall continue until actual realization along with interest and costs.


LIMITATION

The present suit is within time. The Limitation Act, 1963 applies, and Article 47 of the Schedule thereto prescribes a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails; time begins to run from the date of the failure. In the present case, the date of such failure is {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} when the Transaction failed and the refund became due. The suit filed on {{FILING_DATE}} is within the prescribed limitation.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The Plaintiff’s suit is valued at Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-, being the precise amount sought to be recovered, for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The court fee payable is calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act, and the amount due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} (simple interest), on the basis that the Defendant has wrongfully retained the Plaintiff’s money despite the Transaction having failed and refund having become due.

The Plaintiff further claims pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the principal sum of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree.

The Plaintiff also seeks future interest under Section 34 CPC on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and received by the Defendant.
(b) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}.
(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree.
(d) Award future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree until realization.
(e) Award costs of the suit including the pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges of Rs. {{POSTAL_CHARGES}}/-, such costs amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-.
(f) Grant such other relief as this Honourable Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

blocking_issues.issue: Draft contains non-pleading drafting markers (“---SECTION_TEXT---”) which must not appear in a filed plaint
blocking_issues.fix: Delete all occurrences of “---SECTION_TEXT---” and ensure section separation is done by proper headings/spacing only
blocking_issues.location: Between CAUSE OF ACTION and LIMITATION; between INTEREST and PRAYER

blocking_issues.issue: Narrative facts are too generic/skeleton and do not clearly plead the business transaction, agreed obligations, and the specific acts/omissions constituting Defendant’s default
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite the Facts section as a chronological narrative stating (with placeholders where needed) the nature of the proposed business transaction, the purpose and terms of the advance, Defendant’s specific obligations and timelines, what Defendant failed to do, Plaintiff’s follow-ups/termination, and Defendant’s refusal/omission to refund

blocking_issues.issue: Section 65 Indian Contract Act is invoked without pleading the statutory trigger (agreement discovered void / becomes void) or a legally coherent basis for restitution under Section 65 on the pleaded facts
blocking_issues.fix: Amend Legal Basis to: (i) state what Section 65 provides; (ii) plead how, on the pleaded facts, the agreement is treated as having become void / incapable of performance or is discovered void (use {{VOIDNESS_REASON}}/{{TERMINATION_RESCISSION_BASIS}} placeholders if facts are not available); and (iii) plead alternative restitution/unjust enrichment and breach of contract refund without prejudice, while preserving the Section 65 plea as user-requested

blocking_issues.issue: Interest claims are internally inconsistent (pre-suit interest period differs between INTEREST section and PRAYER; pendente lite period in PRAYER is incorrectly anchored to cause of action date rather than date of suit)
blocking_issues.fix: Make interest periods consistent throughout: (i) use a single pre-suit period (e.g., {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} or {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}) consistently in both INTEREST and PRAYER; (ii) correct pendente lite interest to run from {{FILING_DATE}} until date of decree (Section 34 CPC)

blocking_issues.issue: Claim amount is pleaded inconsistently using different numeric formats/figures (Rs 20,00,000 vs Rs 2,000,000) creating ambiguity in the precise amount claimed
blocking_issues.fix: Standardize the principal claim amount throughout the plaint to a single expression, preferably using {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}} consistently (and optionally {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}}) in all sections (Jurisdiction, Facts, Legal Basis, Cause of Action, Limitation, Valuation, Interest, Prayer)
blocking_issues.location: JURISDICTION; FACTS OF THE CASE; LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS; CAUSE OF ACTION; LIMITATION; VALUATION AND COURT FEE; INTEREST; PRAYER

non_blocking_issues.issue: Redundant/awkward party captioning and duplicated heading (“BETWEEN” appears twice; “BETWEEN Plaintiff and Defendant” is non-standard)
non_blocking_issues.fix: Replace with standard cause-title formatting: ‘Between: [Plaintiff details] … Plaintiff’ and ‘And: [Defendant details] … Defendant’

non_blocking_issues.issue: Jurisdiction paragraph includes an unnecessary statement about the Court’s ‘monetary limit’ ({{PECUNIARY_LIMIT}}) which may be incorrect for the forum/state and is not required if suit value is within jurisdiction
non_blocking_issues.fix: Either delete the pecuniary-limit sentence or replace with a neutral pleading that suit valuation is within this Court’s pecuniary jurisdiction as per applicable notifications

non_blocking_issues.issue: Cause of action paragraph is adequate but could be tightened to avoid duplication with limitation and to clearly identify the ‘failure’ date used for Article 47 limitation
non_blocking_issues.fix: Align wording so that the ‘date of failure’ for Article 47 and the first accrual date in Cause of Action are the same defined event/date (e.g., failure of transaction/refund becoming due on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}})

non_blocking_issues.issue: List of Documents numbering starts at 7 instead of 1
non_blocking_issues.fix: Renumber the List of Documents sequentially starting at 1

final_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
final_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

BETWEEN Plaintiff and Defendant. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is the plaintiff herein and is sui juris and competent to sue. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is the defendant herein and is sui juris and competent to be sued.


JURISDICTION

The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} is invoked in this money-recovery suit. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. The advance of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- was paid by the Plaintiff on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} from the Plaintiff's account with {{BANK_NAME}} {{BANK_BRANCH}} and credited to the Defendant in respect of the business transaction/contract evidenced by Annexure A. The Agreement was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_OF_EXECUTION}}. The cause of action first arose when the transaction failed and the refund became due on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and continues due to the Defendant’s ongoing retention of the Plaintiff’s money. The Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- with interest and costs; the suit valuation is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into discussions during {{NEGOTIATION_PERIOD}} for a proposed business transaction, namely {{TRANSACTION_DESCRIPTION}} (hereinafter “the Transaction”). Pursuant to such discussions, on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a written agreement/understanding ("Agreement"), the terms of which are evidenced by Annexure A.

2. Under the Agreement, the Plaintiff agreed to pay an advance of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Defendant towards/for the purpose of {{PURPOSE_OF_ADVANCE}}. The Defendant, in turn, undertook to perform/complete {{DEFENDANT_KEY_OBLIGATIONS}} within the agreed timelines, including by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, and to either (a) complete the Transaction and provide the agreed consideration/benefit to the Plaintiff, or (b) refund the advance if the Transaction did not materialize/failed in terms of the Agreement.

3. In performance of the Plaintiff’s obligation, on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Defendant’s designated account by {{PAYMENT_MODE}} (bank transfer/NEFT/RTGS), as evidenced by Annexure B. The Defendant received the said amount.

4. After receipt of the advance, the Defendant was required to take steps including {{STEPS_REQUIRED_AFTER_ADVANCE}} and to complete performance by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff, acting on the Defendant’s assurances and the Agreement, awaited performance and was ready and willing to complete the Transaction as per the agreed terms.

5. However, on and from {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, the Defendant committed default by {{DEFAULT_PARTICULARS}}. As a result, the Transaction did not materialize and there was a total failure of the consideration/benefit which the Plaintiff was to receive under the Agreement, despite the Plaintiff having already paid the advance.

6. Upon the Transaction failing, the Plaintiff demanded refund of the advance from the Defendant on {{DEMAND_DATE}} by {{DEMAND_MODE}}. Thereafter, the Plaintiff caused a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to be issued to the Defendant demanding refund of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- along with interest and costs (Annexure C). The Defendant has failed and neglected to refund the said amount. Any reply sent by the Defendant, if applicable, is dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D).

Annexure references: Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice), Annexure D (Reply, if any), Annexure E (Acknowledgment/Admission document, if any), Annexure F (Additional supporting documents).


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

A. Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 (as specifically relied upon by the Plaintiff) provides, in substance, that when an agreement is discovered to be void or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement/contract is bound to restore it, or to make compensation for it, to the person from whom he received it.

B. In the present case, the Plaintiff paid Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Defendant as an advance under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A), as evidenced by Annexure B. The Transaction contemplated under the Agreement failed and the Plaintiff did not receive the agreed benefit/consideration due to the Defendant’s default on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} as pleaded above.

C. The Plaintiff pleads that, upon the Transaction failing and upon the Plaintiff’s termination/rescission/withdrawal from the Transaction on {{TERMINATION_DATE}} on account of the Defendant’s default, the Agreement/Transaction stood incapable of being performed/treated as having become void in the circumstances, for reasons including {{VOIDNESS_REASON}} / {{TERMINATION_RESCISSION_BASIS}}. Consequently, the Defendant, having received an advantage (Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-), is bound to restore the same to the Plaintiff under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

D. Without prejudice to the above Section 65 plea, and in the alternative, the Plaintiff is entitled to recovery of the said sum on principles of restitution/unjust enrichment and/or refund consequent to the Defendant’s breach of the Agreement, since the Defendant has retained the Plaintiff’s money despite total failure of the Transaction and despite demand.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform {{DEFENDANT_KEY_OBLIGATIONS}} and, consequently, the Transaction failed and the refund of the advance of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- became due and payable to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff had already paid the said sum on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as evidenced by Annexure B.

The cause of action further accrued on {{DEMAND_DATE}} and on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff demanded refund and caused issuance of legal notice (Annexure C), and the Defendant failed/neglected/refused to refund the amount.

The cause of action is continuing in nature because the Defendant continues to retain Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- belonging to the Plaintiff without lawful justification, and the Plaintiff’s claim remains unpaid as of the date of filing {{FILING_DATE}} and shall continue until actual realization along with interest and costs.


LIMITATION

The present suit is within time. The Limitation Act, 1963 applies, and Article 47 of the Schedule thereto prescribes a period of three years for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails; time begins to run from the date of the failure. In the present case, the date of such failure is {{DEFAULT_DATE}}/{{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} when the Transaction failed and the refund became due. The suit filed on {{FILING_DATE}} is within the prescribed limitation.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The Plaintiff’s suit is valued at Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-, being the precise amount sought to be recovered, for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. The court fee payable is calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act, and the amount due is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} (simple interest), on the basis that the Defendant has wrongfully retained the Plaintiff’s money despite the Transaction having failed and refund having become due.

The Plaintiff further claims pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the principal sum of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree.

The Plaintiff also seeks future interest under Section 34 CPC on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till realization.


PRAYER

(a) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff a sum of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} under the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and received by the Defendant.
(b) Pass a decree directing the Defendant to pay to the Plaintiff pre-suit interest at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}.
(c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}}% per annum on the aforesaid principal sum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree.
(d) Award future interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree until realization.
(e) Award costs of the suit including the pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and postal charges of Rs. {{POSTAL_CHARGES}}/-, such costs amounting to Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}}/-.
(f) Grant such other relief as this Honourable Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.
